ontology.foodex2_description: Sea salt (unrefined salt from evaporated seawater)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 387390002 refers to sodium chloride as a substance; sea salt is predominantly sodium chloride (~98–99%) and this is the closest defensible SNOMED CT ancestor anchor. No exact SNOMED CT code exists for sea salt as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of sea salt is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 6g), used to season a dish for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Sea salt — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database, Food Code F10060047
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database entry for sea salt, food code F10060047. Sodium value of 39,311 mg/100g is consistent with near-pure sodium chloride composition (~98–99% NaCl). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468 (Salt, table) which reports approximately 38,758 mg sodium/100g, confirming plausibility of the HPB figure.

gi_source.title: N/A — Sea salt contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: N/A — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sea salt contains 0g carbohydrates per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as there are no digestible carbohydrates to elicit a glycemic response. Per the Veritas zero-carbohydrate GI rule, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI database entry exists or is needed for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Extreme sodium concentration — 39,311 mg sodium per 100g
hypertension.guidance: Sea salt contains 39,311 mg sodium per 100g, meaning even a single teaspoon (~6g) delivers approximately 2,359 mg sodium — exceeding the AHA daily limit of 2,300 mg; individuals with hypertension must restrict use to a bare pinch and should substitute with herbs, lemon juice, or potassium-based salt alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content but high sodium indirectly worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sea salt does not raise blood glucose directly as it contains no carbohydrates or sugar; however, its extreme sodium content (39,311 mg/100g) can worsen insulin resistance indirectly through hypertension and vascular damage, so use should be kept to a minimum pinch per dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium load increases blood pressure and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: High sodium intake is a primary modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease; sea salt at 39,311 mg sodium/100g poses a significant cardiovascular risk if used liberally, and patients with CVD should limit total daily sodium to under 1,500 mg as per AHA guidelines, making sea salt use extremely restricted.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extreme sodium content exacerbates tissue oedema and joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sea salt at 39,311 mg sodium/100g far exceeds safe thresholds for RA patients; excessive sodium worsens joint swelling and oedema, and high sodium intake has been associated with amplified inflammatory cytokine activity — use should be restricted to a pinch per meal at most.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea salt is a minimally processed seasoning produced by the evaporation of seawater, resulting in white crystalline granules that are predominantly sodium chloride (~98–99% NaCl) with trace amounts of naturally occurring minerals including magnesium, calcium, and potassium. It is available in coarse, medium, and fine crystal forms, offering a distinct texture and slightly briny flavour profile compared to refined table salt. Unlike refined table salt, sea salt undergoes minimal processing and retains small quantities of trace minerals, though these are nutritionally insignificant at culinary quantities. Sea salt provides zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates per 100g, making it a non-caloric seasoning. Its defining nutritional characteristic is an extremely high sodium content of 39,311 mg per 100g — essentially equivalent to refined table salt (~38,758 mg/100g per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468). Despite widespread marketing claims, sea salt is NOT lower in sodium than table salt; both are approximately 98–99% sodium chloride. Clinically, sea salt is a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its extreme sodium density. Even a single teaspoon (~6g) delivers approximately 2,359 mg sodium, exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg. For individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis, sea salt use must be strictly minimised. Glycemic index is not applicable as sea salt contains no digestible carbohydrates. Sea salt is universally used across global cuisines as a flavour enhancer, preservative, and finishing seasoning, often sprinkled on dishes after cooking for texture and taste.
common_user_queries: Is sea salt healthier than table salt?
How much sodium is in sea salt?
Is sea salt bad for high blood pressure?
Sea salt vs table salt sodium content

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (food code F10060047) with 0.95 confidence rating. Sodium value cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468 (Salt, table, ~38,758 mg/100g), confirming plausibility. All core macros are confirmed zero by nature of the food (pure mineral salt with no organic macronutrients). Source match is exact.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — sea salt is an inorganic mineral (sodium chloride) containing no protein. fat_g is naturally zero — sea salt contains no lipids by composition. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — sea salt contains no carbohydrates or sugars. energy_kcal is naturally zero as there are no calorie-yielding macronutrients. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, and cholesterol_mg are all naturally zero. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (F10060047) and consistent with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468. Negative sentinel values (-1): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed vitamin and micromineral data for sea salt are not reported in the HPB Singapore database entry for this food code, and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468 does not provide these values for salt. Glycemic index is not applicable: sea salt contains 0g carbohydrates per 100g; GI is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. GL per serving is 0.0. Sea salt is NOT nutritionally superior to table salt in sodium content despite common marketing claims — both are approximately 98–99% sodium chloride.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains negligible carbohydrates, compliant with the zero-carbohydrate GI rule. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale with organization 'N/A — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate food'. improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed snomed_ct_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' — a valid allowed value per G5 rule set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] — resolving G5 ontology_honesty gate failure.

Iteration 3: Updated nutrient_source URL to the HPB food composition database homepage and clarified title to reference food code F10060047 explicitly, resolving G2 approved-source compliance concern for nutrient source.

Iteration 3: Updated gi_source organization field to 'N/A — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate food' to clearly signal inapplicability without referencing a non-approved source, resolving G2 gi_source compliance failure.
Iteration 3: Replaced USDA cross-reference from NDB 02047 to FDC ID 173468 (current FoodData Central identifier) throughout for accuracy.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_source to document zero-carbohydrate inapplicability. Expanded natural_language_description for Q6 compliance.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Sea salt (F10060047) passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The record is sourced from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (approved primary source, food code F10060047) with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173468. All zero macro values are justified as naturally zero for an inorganic mineral salt. Glycemic index is correctly set to null with gi_category 'not_applicable' per the zero-carbohydrate GI rule. Ontology anchors are defensible: FoodOn exact match (FOODON_03301710), FoodEx2 exact match (A00LC), SNOMED CT closest_parent (387390002) with gap note. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. No mandatory non-English language requirement applies as sea salt is a global universal seasoning.
